text: The beauty of the robot is that it allows me to go in from the right side, lean over to the left and open the left pleura and see the two phrenics, the right phrenic and the left phrenic at the same time.
And it allows me to lift the thymus of the pericardium, allows me to get into the neck, get all the
fat and all the root of the thymus from the neck down into the chest and deliver it out into the chest.
But this is a fairly large one, and we thought this would be a dermoid cyst or a teratoma, but this turned out to be a thymic carcinoid.
This, in my opinion, is one of the most complex surgeries, even by open techniques.
Now these are very, very, very dense adhesions, but the beauty of the robot is it allows you to get into the chest and every single adhesion is taken down under vision.
The key is not to get to the adhesions first, but to get into the hilum, get the bronchus, get the artery, get the vein,
get the bronchus, and then tackle the adhesions.
Because once you've got these structures, then the adhesions are very clearly visualized.
Mind you, though you have taken out all these structures, the adhesions themselves take you about two, two and a half hours to be taken down before you can deliver the specimen out into the chest.
This is what we do.
We use the two needle holders in the two hands.
The needle comes through the chest wall into the chest.
So you don't need to make an extra port to deliver the needle into the chest.
And then you get in there and you do your suturing like you would do a normal plication.
I do this either horizontal or I do this as a circular placation.
It depends on what I find inside.
So, there are various ways to do these placations.
In this particular scenario, I am doing it as a horizontal as a circular placation to get the diaphragm taut back into its normal position.
We went in from the inside, made three small ports, went into the chest, identified the second rib, which is very, very easy to identify once the camera is there.
We dissected the pleura.
We took out the intercostal muscles, identified the rib, and we actually cut the ribs on the two sides and delivered the tumor within the chest.
And once we had delivered it within the chest, we then delivered it out through one of the ports.
The advantage of this was that this guy actually did not need a thoracotomy and within three months he was actually signed off for joining the Indian Navy and I was very, very, very pleased.
We had to innovate in this scenario because we did not have an endoscopic rib cutter.

So what we did was we took a large giggly saw and we passed it around the rib and delivered it out through one of the ports and then we undocked the robot and my assistant used the giggly saw to cut the rib using a two and four movement.
So there we are delivering the giggly saw and then it came out through one of the ports and we are cutting it now with a to and fro moment.
And the same thing was repeated on the other side.
